text: about automation. Automation is the main thing, especially in the enterprise, which is a segment we do exceptionally well. So I think a lot of it is like we place a lot of these vets very early, even with AI, before AI and LLMs were a thing, we were investing already in automation. So I think a lot of this is treading the right balance between how much of it is you bringing customers along to your vision with balancing your roadmap with things they need. And I think companies tend to over-index on one or the other. And having a way of balancing the issue, I think, is actually harder. And AI, I think, is starting now time to probably swing the pendulum a little more into vision land because things are moving so quickly that those will become demands very soon. Yeah, it's like Henry Ford saying, if I ask my customers, they'd say, I want a faster horse, whereas here you're having to rethink the entire model. My question for you, though, is how do you think about bringing that product into existence? Like, do you go to existing customers and go, hey, finance person who is my customer today, you're automated, let me talk to your manager. Like, that doesn't, so how do you think about broaching that? So, the way we thought a lot about it is we have this philosophy of co-designing, which is very strong for the way we build products, which is we don't build product in a vacuum. Like, there isn't really anything that we shipped that we said, hey, let's go build this, make this work for us internally, and then get it out in the world and see what happens. Typically, the way it works is we work with every product with at least 10 to 20, 30 customers, getting into a state that's like even alpha, where we can actually start having any real go-to-market traction demand. And the way we've done it with AI is we actually went to customers that were much farther ahead. Actually, a lot of these are AI companies. And were all 20 or 30 of them, the design partners, current Brex customers? The vast majority are Brex customers. Some are not, but the majority are. I think there's some founders out there thinking about, like, okay, I need to do this sort of work internally at my own company. It's like, okay, do I start with my own customers or do I bring an external? Yeah, I think one mantra that I really believe in is, like, you can sell a new product to your existing customers. You can sell an existing product to a new customer. You cannot sell a new product to a new customer, right? I think it's just too hard to leap in both the product muscle and the go-to-market muscle at the same time. So we tend to over-index on trying to sell these very innovative products to the existing base first So a lot of the know there are actually 200 plus billion dollar company in this cohort by the way as well as like startups that are like 10 20, 30 employees. Yeah. And the reason- 200 billion dollar? There's two 100 plus billion dollar companies. And they're AI, probably AI companies. Yeah. Two AI, actually one smaller one that's not AI. Yeah. There's more retail. And then, but like a very large public company. And then you have 10, 20, 30% startups. And the reason we try to operate with this wide range of customers is because we really believe that customers need to have a single platform across the entire range of growth. There's companies use AWS all the way from two people to Fortune 50. They use Stripe at the same range of scale, and we believe the same should be true with Brex. So we try to anchor on what are the building blocks and primitives that scale throughout your journey as you mature as a company. Yep. And then, so you've got your cohort of prospective customers for this new product, but you don't know what the new product is? We have a decent sense. Or at the very beginning, you're figuring out exactly what it is. So we have a decent sense of the direction we want to go. And then a lot of the way it works is we say, hey, we're going to go and solve this problem for you. Tell us more about the pain. And then it's just traditional product building. It's just like, let us understand why. And we actually try to go really inside a customer and do on-site, speak to people on the ground, like, you know, find ways and, you know, quantifying success. And then really the product building process is like, you know, a lot of the way we talk about it is like there's this space in the journey of building a product where you're operating with an undefined problem, right? It's just like what is the problem that's worth solving? And then a lot of the sort of early co-design phase is like really narrowing on the problem. And once the becomes clear, it can actually move to a much easier stage, which is like the undefined solution stage. And then it's like, okay, how are we actually going to work? What is the design going to be like? What's a good user experience? And a lot of people are actually much better at that once the problem is relatively constrained. So we try to separate these two phases. Defining the problem, I totally agree, is the harder part. Because once you define it, it's also very hard to go back once you start building product and solve it. Yeah. And I think to this point, one of the things that I think we've learned over time is once you start to roll out a product to the vast majority of customers and you have like something like tens of thousands of companies roll out, every product that you ship is 90 of what it will ultimately become So getting that initial phase really dialed in is very very critical So we tend to actually operate with a much longer cycle than people think with these very small cohorts that are just battle-testing things until we actually know and narrow down the concept. And then when the concepts feel right, we can start expanding use cases, the rollout, the number of users. But I think that original genesis of, like, here's the kernel of something great, I think is actually really hard to replicate at scale. I think you have to start really small and, like, and create the kind of environment that helps make that happen. Brex went through YC in, I think, 2018. 17. 17. This reminds me of stock YC advice, which is do things that don't scale and talk to customers. 100%. 100%. So, two of the most. Yeah, and I think the challenge as a company gets bigger is, like, you know, how do you create the mechanisms and systems and incentives that make that happen, right, at scale? And because the physics of product building are the same, it's just, you know, how do you, you know, and I talk a lot about this internally, that the way you measure the quality of a product organization and an engineering organization is, like, the speed between capturing the signal from the customer of, like, there's a problem here, all the way down into we ship the high-quality solution. That actually solves that problem, right? But once you get bigger and there's scale and there's a lot of customers and a lot of users and a lot of employees and a lot of people selling it, right, that becomes trickier. So a lot of the work is how do you make YC-level thinking and quality of operation happen when you have a more much larger. So you're like general zip code, agenda commerce around procurement. You need to get 30 initial customers to find the problem, focus on that. Then the next big thing is how do you put together the team and what is the culture of that team that you built? So I'm curious to hear about the team building side. Yeah, I think for us a very big thing that we realized is, you know, we did this exercise when we started Brex 3.0 like maybe a year and a half ago that really sort of inflected a business, which was, who are the leaders that have been the most successful and the ones that have not? And try to find what is predictive of success. A lot of things are correlated with success, but are also common in those that were not successful. What is just showing up in the folks that have been successful? And we found an attribute that we call operate at all levels which basically means someone that can operate at the higher level like how does this tie to our mission How does this tie to our overall company strategy long range plan financial outcomes all the way down into product design engineering architecture how is this actually getting built on the ground as an IC and people that can actually flex across these levels tend to be much more successful because you start to get bigger, the company starts to operate through proxies, and you lose connectivity with the ground and what you're actually shipping. So we try to run the company much closer to the metal there. So that's the first thing that we really look for in people. And I think it's just the common trait in all the people at Brex that are successful is that ability to switch from 40,000 feet to five in like 10 seconds. The second one is I think there's something about people that are really good that they believe that their work product is a reflection of who they are. So I think a lot of times people say, well, we have to build something with quality or better or all that. And I think people that are great, they see their work as a representation of them. So they tend to hold the bar for themselves and for what they put out in the world much higher than they would originally because they take it personally. And I think the third one is, like, I do think there is something about intensity, which is, you know, I think companies choose to be fast. I think, like, speed and intensity is a choice you make every day. And I think, you know, we can measure the intensity of a company with, like, when you follow up on something, what's an acceptable answer? Like, is it, like, next week, next month, tomorrow, or, like, later today, right? I think if you decide for this team, you'll be in person, in San Francisco, working. Yeah, I mean, we went back in person last year, actually earlier this year, and it's been a very good shift. Yeah. So we're very bullish on that. And then obviously for the AI teams in general, that has been a much bigger component over time that we prioritized more time and exposure in person and very tight collaboration. We do it here and in Seattle. We have a good hub there as well. Our CTO is actually there and I'm here. So it's been a good, and we fly over every couple weeks. So this new product team is 996, but the whole company is not.
